anchor: ChatGPT Pro and Business frame GPT access in ChatGPT as unlimited or effectively unlimited, subject to guardrails.
note: One or more official sources still require a real browser-backed or manual confirmation path before this anchor can be treated as fully current. Methods: openai-pro-plan:ua-fetch-fallback, openai-business-limits:ua-fetch-fallback.

anchor: GPT-5.4 Pro remains the frontier OpenAI model page with a 1,050,000 token context window claim.
note: The latest frontier model page still documents GPT-5.4 Pro and the 1.05M context window. Methods: openai-gpt-5.4-model:direct-fetch.

anchor: This repo should keep GPT-5.4 as the strategic frontier target while treating Codex-surface defaults as a separately verified runtime fact.
note: Official frontier-model and Codex docs can be represented separately: GPT-5.4 stays the strategic frontier target while OpenClaw provider-model promotion remains a separate runtime-probed fact. Methods: openai-gpt-5.4-model:direct-fetch, openai-codex-upgrades:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw provider examples can lag the frontier model naming, so provider-model promotion must be runtime-probed.
note: OpenClaw provider docs still expose Codex-provider example models that can lag the frontier OpenAI model page. Methods: openclaw-model-providers:direct-fetch, openai-gpt-5.4-model:direct-fetch.

anchor: Codex supports ChatGPT sign-in and plan-based access.
note: One or more official sources still require a real browser-backed or manual confirmation path before this anchor can be treated as fully current. Methods: openai-codex-plan:ua-fetch-fallback, openai-codex-upgrades:direct-fetch.

anchor: Codex plan usage windows are separate from ChatGPT chat usage and require pacing discipline.
note: One or more official sources still require a real browser-backed or manual confirmation path before this anchor can be treated as fully current. Methods: openai-codex-plan:ua-fetch-fallback.

anchor: OpenClaw officially supports Codex OAuth or ChatGPT sign-in for external workflows.
note: OpenClaw provider docs still explicitly document OpenAI Codex OAuth support. Methods: openclaw-model-providers:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw recommends Node for stable gateways and does not recommend Bun for gateway installs.
note: The current OpenClaw CLI docs still prefer Node and warn against Bun for stable gateways. Methods: openclaw-cli:direct-fetch.

anchor: The strongest always-on OpenClaw pattern is one primary loopback-bound gateway with secure remote access and remote nodes where needed.
note: OpenClaw docs still recommend one primary gateway with secure remote access and remote nodes where needed. Methods: openclaw-multiple-gateways:direct-fetch, openclaw-remote-access:direct-fetch, openclaw-nodes:direct-fetch.

anchor: Cron and heartbeat serve different purposes and should be combined with explicit wake or continuation flows.
note: The latest automation docs still distinguish cron from heartbeat and support wake-style continuation flows. Methods: openclaw-cron-vs-heartbeat:direct-fetch, openclaw-nodes:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw memory is Markdown-first, and Codex OAuth does not satisfy embeddings for memory search.
note: The latest memory docs still show Markdown-first memory and separate embedding requirements. Methods: openclaw-memory:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw supports QMD and local-first memory search backends.
note: The memory docs still advertise QMD and local-first search options. Methods: openclaw-memory:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw skills, plugins, and skill folders are supply-chain risk and must be treated as trusted or untrusted code accordingly.
note: OpenClaw security guidance still treats skills and plugins as trusted-code or supply-chain risk surfaces. Methods: openclaw-security:direct-fetch, openclaw-secrets:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw current docs expose strict config requirements, additive SecretRefs, and a first-class PDF tool.
note: Current OpenClaw docs still expose config requirements, broader secret coverage, and PDF tooling. Methods: openclaw-configuration:direct-fetch, openclaw-secrets:direct-fetch, openclaw-pdf:direct-fetch.

anchor: OpenClaw can use a managed browser, attached Chrome relay, and remote nodes; headless mode can trigger more anti-bot friction.
note: The browser docs still cover managed browsing, attached Chrome, remote nodes, and headless tradeoffs. Methods: openclaw-browser:direct-fetch, openclaw-browser-login:direct-fetch, openclaw-nodes:direct-fetch, openclaw-faq:direct-fetch.

anchor: Automated self-update is possible but not the primary recommended operating pattern.
note: The current FAQ still treats self-update as possible but not the preferred operating pattern. Methods: openclaw-faq:direct-fetch.

anchor: Wise personal tokens and OAuth 2.0 support different automation realities, and EU or UK PSD2 rules constrain some personal-token actions.
note: Current Wise docs still separate personal-token and OAuth realities and preserve PSD2 constraints. Methods: wise-security-access:direct-fetch, wise-personal-tokens:direct-fetch, wise-getting-started:direct-fetch.

anchor: Steel Cloud and Steel Local differ materially; auth-sensitive credential injection is not a safe assumption in Steel Local.
note: Current Steel docs still distinguish Cloud from Local and separate credential or profile capabilities accordingly. Methods: steel-sessions-overview:direct-fetch, steel-profiles:direct-fetch, steel-credentials:direct-fetch, steel-local-vs-cloud:direct-fetch.
